text: This is a case of total laparoscopic hysterectomy in a young patient with adenomyosis and failure of numerous medical treatments.
The surgeon begins the intervention by controlling the lateral attachments of the uterus, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate and secure these structures.
The surgeon cuts the round ligament of the uterus using surgical scissors, initiating the dissection process in the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
and in this young patient we decide to conserve both ovaries but for decreasing the risk of ovarian cancer we usually don't associate salpingectomy
The surgeon performs a salpingectomy, carefully removing the fallopian tube at a distance from the ovary to preserve the ovarian vascularization.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the utero-ovarian ligament, followed by cutting the ligament with a harmonic ace, demonstrating precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon creates a window to increase the distance between the ureter and the uterus, ensuring safe separation of these structures.

The surgeon performs vesicovaginal dissection by pulling the uterus toward the sacrum and retracting the bladder cranially, progressively opening the vesicovaginal plane until reaching the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator.
The surgeon continues the dissection to clearly expose the anterior aspect of the vagina. The next step involves performing the same procedure on the right side.

The surgeon controls the round ligament approximately one centimeter outside the uterine cornea using the harmonic ace, which cuts through the vascular ligament easily. The peritoneal dissection is then continued.
The surgeon performs a left salpingectomy by cutting the mesosalpinx two centimeters away from the ovary, ensuring the preservation of ovarian vascularization.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate and cut the right utero-ovarian ligament, mirroring the previous steps performed on the left side.

The surgeon controls the uterine pedicle, following dissection on the posterior leaf of the left broad ligament until reaching the upper part of the uterosacral ligament. This maneuver increases uterine mobility, and the uterine pedicle, including the artery and vein, is skeletonized.

The surgeon applies bipolar energy to the entire uterine pedicle without cutting it, while controlling the opposite side to decrease backflow on the right pedicle. The uterus is visibly tracked to the left.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum and applies bipolar energy at the isthmic level of the uterus after skeletalizing the uterine pedicle.

The uterus is lateralized to the left, allowing the surgeon to cut the uterine pedicle, starting with the artery followed by the vein. The same procedure is then performed on the left side, with the uterus being tracked.
The surgeon cuts the uterine pedicle on the right side, which was previously coagulated, ensuring a bloodless procedure. The vein, located medial to the artery, is being cut.

The surgeon performs a colpotomy using a monopolar needle, beginning the incision on the anterior aspect of the vagina. The section is then extended to the left lateral aspect, with clear visibility of both anterior and posterior vaginal walls.
uterine manipulator valve.
The surgeon cuts the right lateral aspect of the vagina, followed by the posterior aspect, completing the section under clear visual control.
The surgeon extracts the uterus vaginally, with the vaginal edges visible and no bipolar hemostasis being applied.
The surgeon performs hemostasis by placing stitches on the surgical site, ensuring control of any potential bleeding.
The surgeon uses a Vicryl O stitch to secure the tissue, employing a Rodol knot, which is an autoblocking and quick-tying knot.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator valve to control the closure of the vaginal cuff.
The surgeon places the first stitches, ensuring both utero-sacral ligaments are included to reduce the risk of post-operative vaginal prolapse.
The surgeon uses interrupted sutures to close the vaginal cuff, avoiding a running suture technique.
The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using four or five interrupted stitches, avoiding a running suture to prevent ischemia.
The surgeon ties the final knot using either intracorporeal or extracorporeal knotting techniques to close the vaginal cuff, minimizing the use of energy for hemostasis.
The surgeon confirms the complete closure of the vagina and proceeds to visually inspect the right ureter, clearly observing the ureteral reptation on both the right and left sides.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy for ovarian conservation, fixing the ovary to the homolateral round ligament to decrease the risk of ovarian torsion. The same procedure is planned for the right side.

The surgeon passes the needle through the ovary and the right round ligament, taking care to avoid damaging the external iliac vessel during this step of the total laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
